section.title: Cyrus, the Lord's Anointed
section.sectionRationale: This passage contains one of the boldest predictive claims in all of Scripture: the LORD names Cyrus by name as His anointed shepherd who will rebuild Jerusalem and free the exiles, more than a century before Cyrus was born. It presses to its sharpest point the Book of Comfort's whole argument that only the true God can declare the end from the beginning, and it closes with an astonishing invitation for every nation on earth to turn to Him and be saved.
section.thesis: The LORD who alone declares the end from the beginning names a pagan king, Cyrus, as His anointed instrument to free His exiled people and rebuild His city, proving there is no God besides Him, and He caps this proof with an open invitation for every knee on earth to bow to Him alone.
section.frameNote: Eight movements: the LORD as maker of all things who confirms His servants' words; the naming of Cyrus as shepherd even before his birth; the LORD's grasp of Cyrus's right hand to subdue nations; Cyrus called by name though he does not know the LORD; the folly of the clay quarreling with its potter; nations submitting and confessing God is with Israel alone; the LORD's open, undisguised speech; and the climactic invitation for all the earth to turn and be saved.

units.label: The LORD who made all things

verses.text: Thus says the LORD, your Redeemer who formed you from the womb: “I am the LORD, who has made all things, who alone stretched out the heavens, who by Myself spread out the earth,
who foils the signs of false prophets and makes fools of diviners, who confounds the wise and turns their knowledge into nonsense,

who confirms the message of His servant and fulfills the counsel of His messengers, who says of Jerusalem, ‘She will be inhabited,’ and of the cities of Judah, ‘They will be rebuilt, and I will restore their ruins,’

units.label: Who says of Cyrus, My shepherd

verses.text: who says to the depths of the sea, ‘Be dry, and I will dry up your currents,’
who says of Cyrus, ‘My shepherd will fulfill all that I desire,’ who says of Jerusalem, ‘She will be rebuilt,’ and of the temple, ‘Let its foundation be laid.’”

This is what the LORD says to Cyrus His {{mashiach|anointed}}, whose right hand I have grasped to subdue nations before him, to disarm kings, to open the doors before him, so that the gates will not be shut:
“I will go before you and level the mountains; I will break down the gates of bronze and cut through the bars of iron.
I will give you the treasures of darkness and the riches hidden in secret places, so that you may know that I am the LORD, the God of Israel, who calls you by name.

units.label: For the sake of Jacob My servant
units.note: though you have not known Me

verses.text: For the sake of Jacob My servant and Israel My chosen one, I call you by name; I have given you a title of honor, though you have not known Me.
I am the LORD, and there is no other; there is no God but Me. I will equip you for battle, though you have not known Me,
so that all may know, from where the sun rises to where it sets, that there is none but Me; I am the LORD, and there is no other.
I form the light and create the darkness; I bring prosperity and create calamity. I, the LORD, do all these things.

units.label: Woe to him who quarrels with his Maker

verses.text: Drip down, O heavens, from above, and let the skies pour down righteousness. Let the earth open up that salvation may sprout and righteousness spring up with it; I, the LORD, have created it.
Woe to him who quarrels with his {{yotser|Maker}}, one clay pot among many. Does the clay ask the potter, ‘What are you making?’ Does your work say, ‘He has no hands’?
Woe to him who says to his father, ‘What have you begotten?’ or to his mother, ‘What have you brought forth?’”
Thus says the LORD, the Holy One of Israel, and its Maker: “How dare you question Me about My sons, or instruct Me in the work of My hands?
It is I who made the earth and created man upon it. It was My hands that stretched out the heavens, and I ordained all their host.
I will raise up Cyrus in righteousness, and I will make all his ways straight. He will rebuild My city and set My exiles free, but not for payment or reward, says the LORD of Hosts.”

units.label: Nations will come over to you

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “The products of Egypt and the merchandise of Cush, along with the Sabeans, men of stature, will come over to you and will be yours; they will trudge behind you; they will come over in chains and bow down to you. They will confess to you: ‘God is indeed with you, and there is no other; there is no other God.’”
Truly You are a God who hides Himself, O God of Israel, the Savior.
They will all be put to shame and humiliated; the makers of idols will depart together in disgrace.
But Israel will be saved by the LORD with an everlasting salvation; you will not be put to shame or humiliated, to ages everlasting.

units.label: I have not spoken in secret

verses.text: For thus says the LORD, who created the heavens, He is God; He formed the earth and fashioned it; He established it; He did not create it to be empty, but formed it to be inhabited: “I am the LORD, and there is no other.
I have not spoken in secret, from a place in a land of darkness. I did not say to the descendants of Jacob, ‘Seek Me in a wasteland.’ I, the LORD, speak the truth; I say what is right.

units.label: Turn to Me and be saved

verses.text: Come, gather together, and draw near, you fugitives from the nations. Ignorant are those who carry idols of wood and pray to a god that cannot save.

Speak up and present your case, yes, let them take counsel together. Who foretold this long ago? Who announced it from ancient times? Was it not I, the LORD? There is no other God but Me, a righteous God and Savior; there is none but Me.
Turn to Me and be saved, all the ends of the earth; for I am God, and there is no other.
By Myself I have sworn; truth has gone out from My mouth, a word that will not be revoked: Every knee will bow before Me, every tongue will swear allegiance.
Surely they will say of Me, ‘In the LORD alone are righteousness and strength.’” All who rage against Him will come to Him and be put to shame.
In the LORD all descendants of Israel will be justified and will exult.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one, one set apart and empowered for a God-given task, the same word that becomes the title Messiah. It is striking that Isaiah applies this word to Cyrus, a pagan Persian king who does not know the LORD, showing that God can anoint even an unbelieving ruler as His chosen instrument, while the fullest and final Anointed One remains Jesus the Christ.

yotser.gloss: Potter, one who forms or shapes, from the verb yatsar, to form or fashion, the same word used of God forming Adam from the dust. The image of the clay quarreling with its potter exposes the absurdity of any creature questioning its Maker's right to shape history, including raising up a foreign king for His own redemptive purposes.

basic.context: In one of the most striking predictive claims in the Bible, the LORD names Cyrus, a Persian king who would not be born for roughly another hundred and fifty years, calling him His shepherd and even His anointed, the one who will free the exiles and see Jerusalem and its temple rebuilt. The passage builds this astonishing claim into an argument that only the true God can declare the future, closing with an open invitation for every nation on earth to turn to Him and be saved.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage names Cyrus with startling specificity long before his birth, and Cyrus's later decree, recorded in Ezra 1:1-4, releasing the Jewish exiles to return and rebuild the temple, matches this prophecy closely. The chapter's repeated insistence that the LORD alone declares the end from the beginning is the very claim this specific, named prophecy is meant to prove beyond doubt.
basic.originalLanguages: Remarkably, Cyrus is called the LORD's {{mashiach}}, anointed one, though he was a pagan king who, the text says twice, did not know the LORD, showing that God's anointing can rest even on someone outside the covenant to accomplish His purposes for His people. The image of the clay questioning the {{yotser}}, potter, underscores that no creature, and no foreign king, has standing to challenge the Maker's right to direct history as He wills.
basic.typology: Cyrus, an anointed deliverer who frees captives and enables the rebuilding of God's city though he is not himself part of God's covenant people, foreshadows in a partial and imperfect way the true and final Anointed One, Jesus the Messiah, who accomplishes a far greater deliverance and gathers a people not limited to any one nation. The invitation for all the ends of the earth to turn and be saved, verse 22, anticipates the global reach of the gospel.
basic.christ: Paul directly applies Isaiah 45:23, 'every knee will bow before Me, every tongue will swear allegiance,' to Jesus Christ in Philippians 2:10-11, declaring that at the name of Jesus every knee will bow and every tongue confess Him as Lord, identifying Jesus fully with the LORD who speaks these words. Cyrus, though merely a human instrument and not himself a believer, becomes an unlikely, partial picture of the Anointed One who frees captives, while Jesus alone is the true Messiah in whom this passage's invitation to all the earth is fully realized.
basic.god: God governs history so completely that He can name a specific foreign king more than a century before his birth and call him His own shepherd and anointed, even though that king does not know Him. He is also the potter with sovereign right over every part of His creation, and He is, remarkably, a God who hides Himself yet still speaks plainly and truly, inviting the whole earth, not Israel alone, to turn to Him and be saved.
basic.crossReferences: Ezra 1:1-4 records Cyrus's actual decree freeing the exiles and funding the temple's rebuilding, fulfilling this prophecy. Philippians 2:10-11 applies Isaiah 45:23 directly to Jesus, every knee bowing and every tongue confessing Him as Lord. Romans 14:11 also quotes this verse regarding final accountability before God. Ezra 1:1 itself explicitly credits the LORD with stirring Cyrus's spirit to fulfill Isaiah's word.
basic.oneStory: This passage's astonishing naming of Cyrus, and the LORD's claim to be the only one who can declare the end from the beginning, becomes the platform for an invitation that reaches beyond Israel to all the ends of the earth. That invitation, and the verse that seals it, is applied directly to Jesus in the New Testament, showing that the God who directed a pagan king's rise is the same Lord before whom every knee will one day bow.

context.literary: An extended oracle in eight movements built around the naming of Cyrus, framed by declarations of the LORD's unrivaled power over creation and history, and closing with a universal invitation and a sworn, unbreakable oath.
context.historical: Cyrus the Great founded the Persian Empire and conquered Babylon in 539 BC; his subsequent decree, recorded in Ezra 1:1-4 and confirmed by the Cyrus Cylinder, an ancient Persian artifact, allowed captive peoples, including the Jewish exiles, to return home and rebuild their temples, matching this prophecy's specific claims.
context.canonical: The climactic center of Isaiah's sustained argument, chapters 40 to 48, that only the true God can declare the future, and the source of Philippians 2:10-11's universal confession of Christ's lordship, one of the New Testament's most direct applications of an Isaiah text to Jesus.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To prove decisively, through the specific naming of a still-future king, that the LORD alone governs and declares history, and to use that proof as grounds for inviting not only Israel but every nation on earth to turn to Him and be saved.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific historical prophecy and its fulfillment through Cyrus; it is not a template for expecting every prayer or plan to name specific future rulers. Its abiding principle is that God alone knows and directs the future, and that salvation is offered freely to all who turn to Him, not earned by any nation's status.

debates.question: How should readers understand Cyrus being named more than a century before his birth, and what does this imply for the composition of the book of Isaiah?

views.label: A single prophet, genuine predictive prophecy
views.case: The traditional view holds that the prophet Isaiah, writing in the eighth century BC under divine inspiration, genuinely named Cyrus generations in advance, exactly the kind of specific predictive prophecy this very passage claims only the true God can give, consistent with the New Testament's practice of citing material from across the whole book as 'Isaiah,' John 12:38-41.

views.label: A later prophet writing during or near the exile
views.case: Many modern scholars hold that chapters 40 to 55, sometimes called Second Isaiah, were composed by a later, unnamed prophet writing during or close to the Babylonian exile, addressing Cyrus as a figure already rising to power or recently victorious, with this material later joined to the earlier Isaiah's scroll.

debates.bearing: This debate should be held with real humility, since it touches questions of authorship history that faithful readers have answered differently; what the text itself insists on, and what this study affirms, is its own repeated claim that only the true God, not any idol, can declare the end from the beginning, Isaiah 46:10, a claim this passage's naming of Cyrus is specifically designed to prove.

study.originalLanguages: Cyrus is astonishingly called the LORD's {{mashiach}}, anointed one, even as a pagan king who does not know Him, showing that God's anointing for a task can extend beyond the covenant people themselves. The image of the clay questioning its {{yotser}}, potter, underlines that no one, whether Israel or a foreign king, has any standing to dispute the Maker's sovereign right to direct the course of history.

typology.type: Cyrus, an anointed deliverer who frees captives though he is not himself God's covenant people
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the true Messiah, whose deliverance and lordship extend to people from every nation, Philippians 2:10-11.

typology.type: The invitation for all the ends of the earth to turn and be saved
typology.fulfillment: The global reach of the gospel, offered to every nation through Christ.

study.christ: Paul applies Isaiah 45:23 directly to Jesus in Philippians 2:10-11, declaring that God has exalted Jesus 'so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord,' identifying Jesus fully with the LORD who swears this oath. Romans 14:11 likewise applies this verse to final accountability before God. Cyrus, though merely a pagan instrument, becomes an unlikely foreshadowing of the true Anointed One, who alone brings the complete and final deliverance this passage's language gestures toward.
study.god: God governs history so thoroughly that He can name a specific foreign king more than a century in advance and call him His own shepherd and anointed, though that king does not know Him. He is sovereign potter over every part of His creation, and though He is, in a real sense, a God who hides Himself, He still speaks plainly and truthfully, extending an open invitation to all the ends of the earth, not Israel alone, to turn to Him and be saved.

crossReferences.note: Records Cyrus's actual decree freeing the exiles and funding the rebuilding of the temple, explicitly crediting the LORD with stirring his spirit.
Applies Isaiah 45:23 directly to Jesus, every knee bowing and every tongue confessing Him as Lord.
Also quotes Isaiah 45:23 regarding every person's final accountability before God.
Continues this passage's claim that only the LORD declares the end from the beginning, the very claim this Cyrus prophecy is designed to prove.

study.oneStory: This passage's astonishing naming of Cyrus, and the LORD's insistence that He alone can declare the end from the beginning, becomes the platform for an invitation reaching beyond Israel to every nation on earth. The verse that seals that invitation is applied directly to Jesus in the New Testament, showing that the same God who directed a pagan king's rise is the Lord before whom every knee will one day bow.

quiz.q: What specific claim does the LORD make about Cyrus in 44:28?

options.text: That Cyrus is His shepherd who will fulfill all His purposes, including rebuilding Jerusalem and its temple.
options.note: Verse 28 states this specific naming and purpose directly, well before Cyrus's birth.

options.text: That Cyrus will become a worshiper of the LORD from birth.
options.note: The passage later says Cyrus does not know the LORD, verses 4 to 5, even though he serves God's purpose.

options.text: That Cyrus will destroy Jerusalem completely.
options.note: The opposite; Cyrus is named as the one who will see Jerusalem and the temple rebuilt, not destroyed.

options.text: That Cyrus will rule only over Persia with no wider influence.
options.note: The passage describes Cyrus subduing nations and disarming kings, far beyond ruling Persia alone.

quiz.q: What striking title is given to Cyrus in 45:1, despite his not knowing the LORD?

options.text: The LORD's anointed.
options.note: Verse 1 gives Cyrus this title, mashiach, remarkable for a pagan king.

options.text: A false prophet to be avoided.
options.note: The opposite; Cyrus is named as God's chosen instrument, not a false prophet.

options.text: An enemy of God's people to be destroyed.
options.note: The passage presents Cyrus as an instrument of deliverance for God's people, not their enemy.

options.text: A rival god to be worshiped alongside the LORD.
options.note: Cyrus is presented as a human instrument used by the one true God, not a rival deity.

quiz.q: What image does verse 9 use to describe the folly of questioning God's purposes?

options.text: Clay quarreling with the potter who shapes it.
options.note: Verse 9 uses exactly this image of the absurdity of clay questioning its maker.

options.text: A shepherd questioning his sheep.
options.note: The image used is the potter and clay, not a shepherd and sheep.

options.text: A king questioning his own soldiers.
options.note: Not the image used here, which specifically concerns a craftsman and the material he shapes.

options.text: A father questioning his own reflection.
options.note: The image given is specifically the potter and the clay he forms, illustrating the Maker-creature relationship.

quiz.q: What universal invitation closes this passage in verse 22?

options.text: Turn to Me and be saved, all the ends of the earth.
options.note: Verse 22 issues this invitation directly to every nation, not Israel alone.

options.text: Only Israel is invited to turn and be saved.
options.note: The opposite; the invitation is explicitly extended to 'all the ends of the earth.'

options.text: Salvation is limited to those who first defeat their enemies in battle.
options.note: The invitation is simply to turn to the LORD, not a military condition for salvation.

options.text: The invitation applies only to future generations, not those currently living.
options.note: The invitation is addressed directly and immediately, 'turn to Me and be saved,' not deferred to a future generation only.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament apply Isaiah 45:23, 'every knee will bow before Me'?

options.text: Paul applies it directly to Jesus, declaring every knee will bow at His name, Philippians 2:10-11.
options.note: Philippians 2:10-11 quotes this very language and applies it to Christ's lordship.

options.text: The New Testament never references this verse.
options.note: The opposite; Paul directly applies this verse to Jesus in Philippians 2, and again in Romans 14:11.

options.text: It is applied only to angelic beings, not to any human or divine figure.
options.note: Paul applies it specifically to Jesus Christ, not to angels.

options.text: It is applied only to Old Testament kings, with no New Testament significance.
options.note: Its clearest New Testament application is directly to Jesus Christ, not to Old Testament kings.

questions: The LORD names Cyrus by name more than a century before his birth. What does this level of detailed foreknowledge tell you about how much God already knows and has planned for your own life?
Cyrus served God's purposes even though he did not know the LORD personally. Where might God be using people or circumstances in your life for good, even ones that do not consciously acknowledge Him?

The image of clay questioning its potter warns against disputing God's right to direct history. Is there a decision or circumstance in your life right now where you are quarreling with your Maker instead of trusting Him?
The invitation 'turn to Me and be saved' is addressed to all the ends of the earth, not a select few. Who in your life needs to hear this open invitation from you this week?
Paul applies 'every knee will bow' directly to Jesus. How does picturing every knee, including your own, one day bowing before Christ change the way you live and worship today?

translationNotes.note: Cyrus the Great, founder of the Persian Empire, conquered Babylon in 539 BC and issued a decree recorded in Ezra 1:1-4 releasing captive peoples, including the Jewish exiles, matching this prophecy's specific claims about Jerusalem and the temple.

'I form the light and create the darkness, I bring prosperity and create calamity' asserts God's sovereignty over all events without making Him the author of moral evil; the parallel term to prosperity is calamity or disaster, not moral wickedness.
Where BSB prints an em dash in this verse, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 44:24-45:25. Where BSB printed an em dash in 45:9 it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
